html:
[1]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 钦定四库全书 - Imperially designated Four Treasuries, a monumental collection of Chinese literature and scholarship.
  • 子部十二 - Subdivision Twelve, a specific classification within the Chinese library system.
  • 提要 - Abstract or summary, typically found at the beginning of a book or chapter.
  • 朝野佥载 - Collective Records of the Court and the People, a historical record of anecdotes and events from the Tang dynasty.
  • 张鷟 - Zhang Zuo, a notable figure in the Tang dynasty, known for his literary works.
  • 新唐书艺文志 - Literary Records of the New History of the Tang Dynasty, a historical record detailing the literary works of the Tang dynasty.
  • 宋史艺文志 - Literary Records of the History of Song, a historical record detailing the literary works of the Song dynasty.
  • 文献通考 - Comprehensive Examination of Literature and History, a comprehensive historical and cultural record.
  • 开元中 - During the Kaiyuan period, a significant period in the Tang dynasty, marked by cultural and economic prosperity.
  • 姚崇 - Yao Chong, a prominent statesman and chancellor during the Tang dynasty.
  • 奉使江南 - Sent as an envoy to Jiangnan, a diplomatic mission to the southern regions of China.
  • 受遗赐死 - Accused of accepting a legacy and sentenced to death, a serious accusation.
  • 上表请代 - Submitted a petition to be substituted, a request to take someone else's place in a punishment.
  • 减死流岭南 - Reduced to exile to Lingnan instead of execution, a lesser punishment.
  • 数年起为长史 - After several years, appointed as the Chief Clerk, a promotion.
  • 资阳石走 - The stone of Ziyang moved, a reference to a historical event.
  • 资阳 - Ziyang, a place name.
  • 孟宏微对宣宗 - Meng Hongwei's response to Emperor Xuanzong, a historical reference.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 钦定四库全书 - A monumental collection of Chinese literature and scholarship.
  • 子部十二 - A specific classification within the Chinese library system.
  • 提要 - A common feature in Chinese books, providing a summary or introduction.
  • 朝野佥载 - A historical record of anecdotes and events from the Tang dynasty.
  • 张鷟 - A notable figure in the Tang dynasty, known for his literary works.
  • 新唐书艺文志 - A historical record detailing the literary works of the Tang dynasty.
  • 宋史艺文志 - A historical record detailing the literary works of the Song dynasty.
  • 文献通考 - A comprehensive historical and cultural record.
  • 开元中 - A significant period in the Tang dynasty, marked by cultural and economic prosperity.
  • 姚崇 - A prominent statesman and chancellor during the Tang dynasty.
  • 奉使江南 - A diplomatic mission to the southern regions of China.
  • 受遗赐死 - A serious accusation of accepting a legacy and being sentenced to death.
  • 上表请代 - A request to take someone else's place in a punishment.
  • 减死流岭南 - A lesser punishment, exile to the southern regions.
  • 数年起为长史 - A promotion to a higher position after several years.
  • 资阳石走 - A historical event involving the movement of a stone in Ziyang.
  • 资阳 - A place name, Ziyang.
  • 孟宏微对宣宗 - A historical reference to a response given to Emperor Xuanzong.

Proper Nouns

  • 钦定四库全书 - Imperially designated Four Treasuries
  • 子部十二 - Subdivision Twelve
  • 提要 - Abstract or summary
  • 朝野佥载 - Collective Records of the Court and the People
  • 张鷟 - Zhang Zuo
  • 新唐书艺文志 - Literary Records of the New History of the Tang Dynasty
  • 宋史艺文志 - Literary Records of the History of Song
  • 文献通考 - Comprehensive Examination of Literature and History
  • 开元中 - During the Kaiyuan period
  • 姚崇 - Yao Chong
  • 奉使江南 - Sent as an envoy to Jiangnan
  • 受遗赐死 - Accused of accepting a legacy and sentenced to death
  • 上表请代 - Submitted a petition to be substituted
  • 减死流岭南 - Reduced to exile to Lingnan instead of execution
  • 数年起为长史 - After several years, appointed as the Chief Clerk
  • 资阳 - Ziyang
  • 孟宏微 - Meng Hongwei
  • 宣宗 - Emperor Xuanzong

[2]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 事时代皆不相及 - Literally means 'the times and eras do not correspond,' suggesting that the content and era of the text are not aligned.
  • 耳目所接可据者多 - Means that there are many reliable and verifiable accounts.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 尤袤 - A scholar and poet of the Song Dynasty known for his bibliographic works.
  • 遂初堂 - The name of You Mao's study or library.
  • 朝野佥载 - A historical record compiled during the Tang Dynasty, known for its anecdotes and stories.
  • 陈振孙 - A scholar of the Song Dynasty known for his bibliographic catalog.
  • 洪迈 - A scholar and writer of the Song Dynasty, known for his 'Yongzai Suibi.'
  • 司马光 - A prominent historian of the Song Dynasty, best known for compiling the 'Zizhi Tongjian.'
  • 乾隆四十六年 - The 46th year of the Qianlong Emperor's reign, corresponding to 1781 CE.

Proper Nouns

  • 尤袤 - You Mao, a Song Dynasty scholar and poet.
  • 遂初堂 - Suichutang, You Mao's study or library.
  • 朝野佥载 - Chao Ye Qian Zai, a historical record from the Tang Dynasty.
  • 陈振孙 - Chen Zhensun, a Song Dynasty scholar.
  • 洪迈 - Hong Mai, a Song Dynasty scholar and writer.
  • 司马光 - Sima Guang, a Song Dynasty historian.
  • 乾隆四十六年 - The 46th year of the Qianlong Emperor's reign (1781 CE).

[3]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 總纂官 - Title meaning 'chief compiler' or 'chief editor', indicating a high-ranking official responsible for overseeing the compilation of a text.
  • 總校官 - Title meaning 'chief proofreader' or 'chief reviser', indicating a high-ranking official responsible for reviewing and correcting the text.
  • 欽定 - Term meaning 'imperial decree' or 'royally sanctioned', indicating that the text is officially endorsed by the emperor.
  • 朝野佥载 - Title meaning 'Records of the Court and the World', referring to a historical text documenting events and anecdotes from both the court and the general populace.
  • 贞观年中 - Time phrase meaning 'during the Zhenguan period', referring to the era of Emperor Taizong of Tang (627–649).
  • 定州鼓城县人 - Phrase meaning 'a person from Gu County, Ding Prefecture', providing the geographical and administrative context of the subject.
  • 问卜者 - Phrase meaning 'a fortune-teller' or 'a diviner', referring to someone who predicts the future or interprets omens.
  • 犁耳 - Term meaning 'plow ear', referring to a part of the plow, which is used metaphorically in the text.
  • 曲桑 - Term meaning 'crooked mulberry tree', referring to a specific type of tree mentioned in the text.
  • 周郎中裴珪 - Title and name meaning 'Péi Guī, the Langzhong of Zhou', referring to a high-ranking official.
  • 妾赵氏 - Phrase meaning 'mistress Zhao', referring to a concubine.
  • 美色 - Term meaning 'beautiful appearance' or 'good looks', describing a person's physical attractiveness.
  • 卜年命 - Phrase meaning 'predicting one's destiny', referring to the practice of fortune-telling.
  • 猪视者 - Term meaning 'pig-eyed', referring to a specific type of eye shape in traditional Chinese physiognomy.
  • 淫妇人 - Term meaning 'promiscuous woman', describing a woman with a bad reputation for sexual behavior.
  • 四白 - Term meaning 'four whites', referring to the white parts of the eye in traditional Chinese physiognomy.
  • 五夫守宅 - Phrase meaning 'five husbands protecting the home', a traditional belief in Chinese culture.
  • 奸废 - Term meaning 'degradation due to adultery', referring to the consequences of immoral behavior.
  • 慎之 - Phrase meaning 'be cautious', advising someone to be careful.
  • 与人奸 - Phrase meaning 'commit adultery with someone', describing an act of infidelity.
  • 没入掖庭 - Phrase meaning 'sent to the imperial harem', indicating a severe punishment for immoral behavior.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 贞观年中 - Refers to the Zhenguan period (627–649), a golden age of the Tang Dynasty under Emperor Taizong.
  • 定州鼓城县 - Refers to Gu County, a county in Ding Prefecture, providing the geographical and administrative context of the events.
  • 张鷟 - Refers to Zhang Zuo, a prominent Tang Dynasty writer and official known for his literary works.
  • 魏全 - Refers to Wei Quan, a person from Gu County, Ding Prefecture, who is the subject of the first anecdote.
  • 王子贞 - Refers to Wang Zhen, a fortune-teller who provides a diagnosis for Wei Quan's mother.
  • 犁耳 - Refers to a part of the plow, which is used metaphorically in the text.
  • 曲桑 - Refers to a specific type of mulberry tree mentioned in the text.
  • 周郎中裴珪 - Refers to Péi Guī, a high-ranking official during the Tang Dynasty.
  • 妾赵氏 - Refers to Mistress Zhao, a concubine of Péi Guī.
  • 张璟藏 - Refers to Zhang Jingcang, a fortune-teller who predicts the future of Mistress Zhao.

Proper Nouns

  • 总纂官 - Chief compiler
  • 总校官 - Chief proofreader
  • 纪昀 - Ji Yun
  • 陆锡熊 - Lu Xixiong
  • 孙士毅 - Sun Shiyi
  • 陆费墀 - Lu Feichi
  • 欽定四库全书 - The Four Treasuries (officially endorsed)
  • 朝野佥载 - Records of the Court and the World
  • 贞观年中 - During the Zhenguan period
  • 定州鼓城县 - Gu County, Ding Prefecture
  • 魏全 - Wei Quan
  • 王子贞 - Wang Zhen
  • 犁耳 - Plow ear
  • 曲桑 - Crooked mulberry tree
  • 周郎中裴珪 - Péi Guī, the Langzhong of Zhou
  • 妾赵氏 - Mistress Zhao
  • 美色 - Beautiful appearance
  • 张璟藏 - Zhang Jingcang
  • 猪视者 - Pig-eyed
  • 淫妇人 - Promiscuous woman
  • 四白 - Four whites
  • 五夫守宅 - Five husbands protecting the home
  • 奸废 - Degradation due to adultery
  • 慎之 - Be cautious
  • 与人奸 - Commit adultery with someone
  • 没入掖庭 - Sent to the imperial harem

[4]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 垂拱中 - A time period during the reign of Emperor Gaozong of Tang (684-685 AD).
  • 平章事 - A high-ranking official position, equivalent to a chancellor or prime minister.
  • 廷诤 - To argue or debate in court.
  • 左授 - To demote or transfer to a lower position, often to a distant place.
  • 三品 - A rank in the imperial bureaucracy, indicating a high-level official.
  • 紫袍 - A purple robe, a symbol of high rank and honor.
  • 夏中服紫衫而终 - Wearing a purple shirt in summer and dying, suggesting a premature death.
  • 瀛州 - A prefecture in ancient China, located in modern-day Hebei Province.
  • 安縣令 - The county magistrate of An County.
  • 沧州弓高令 - The county magistrate of Gonggao County in Cang Prefecture.
  • 九宫推算 - A method of divination using the nine palaces of the Luo Shu (a magic square).
  • 天罡临命 - A term in Chinese astrology indicating a significant influence on one's fate.
  • 周易筮之 - Divination using the *I Ching* (Book of Changes).
  • 遇观之涣 - Encountering the hexagram *View* (观) and *Scattering* (涣) in a divination.
  • 主惊恐 - Indicating fear or anxiety.
  • 风水行上事 - A term related to geomancy or feng shui, suggesting a favorable or unfavorable situation.
  • 安国观道士 - A Taoist priest at An Guo Temple.
  • 不告姓名 - Not revealing one's name.
  • 暗使推之 - Secretly instructing someone to perform divination.
  • 身在天牢 - Being imprisoned in the heavenly jail, a metaphor for severe punishment.
  • 大辟之罪 - A capital crime.
  • 病当死 - Will die of illness.
  • 无救法 - No remedy.
  • 被御史李全交致其罪 - Accused by the censor Li Quan Jiao.
  • 信有征矣 - Indeed, there is evidence.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 杜景佺 - A historical figure, a Tang dynasty official known for his integrity and administrative skills.
  • 信都 - A place name, modern-day Xingtai, Hebei Province.
  • 则天 - Empress Wu Zetian, the only female emperor in Chinese history.
  • 溱州 - A remote prefecture, likely in modern-day Sichuan or Yunnan Province.
  • 张鷟 - A famous Tang dynasty writer and official.
  • 梁州 - A prefecture in ancient China, located in modern-day Shaanxi Province.
  • 道士 - Taoist priests.
  • 九宫 - The nine palaces of the Luo Shu, a fundamental concept in Chinese numerology and divination.
  • 天罡临命 - A term in Chinese astrology indicating a significant influence on one's fate.
  • 周易 - The *I Ching* (Book of Changes), an ancient Chinese divination text.
  • 观之涣 - Hexagrams in the *I Ching*, *View* (观) and *Scattering* (涣).
  • 主惊恐 - Indicating fear or anxiety.
  • 风水 - Feng shui, a traditional Chinese practice of harmonizing individuals with their environment.
  • 安国观 - A Taoist temple.
  • 李全交 - A censor in the Tang dynasty.
  • 刑部尚书 - The Minister of Justice.
  • 李知白 - A high-ranking official in the Tang dynasty.
  • 张廷珪 - A high-ranking official in the Tang dynasty.
  • 程行谋 - A high-ranking official in the Tang dynasty.
  • 岭南 - A region in southern China, including modern-day Guangdong, Guangxi, and Hainan provinces.

Proper Nouns

  • 杜景佺 - A Tang dynasty official.
  • 信都 - A place name, modern-day Xingtai, Hebei Province.
  • 则天 - Empress Wu Zetian.
  • 溱州 - A remote prefecture.
  • 张鷟 - A Tang dynasty writer and official.
  • 梁州 - A prefecture in ancient China.
  • 道士 - Taoist priests.
  • 九宫 - The nine palaces of the Luo Shu.
  • 天罡临命 - A term in Chinese astrology.
  • 周易 - The *I Ching* (Book of Changes).
  • 观之涣 - Hexagrams in the *I Ching*.
  • 主惊恐 - Indicating fear or anxiety.
  • 风水 - Feng shui.
  • 安国观 - A Taoist temple.
  • 李全交 - A censor in the Tang dynasty.
  • 刑部尚书 - The Minister of Justice.
  • 李知白 - A high-ranking official in the Tang dynasty.
  • 张廷珪 - A high-ranking official in the Tang dynasty.
  • 程行谋 - A high-ranking official in the Tang dynasty.
  • 岭南 - A region in southern China.

[5]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 大疯 - Severe skin disease, likely leprosy, in the context of the Tang Dynasty.
  • 蚺蛇胆 - Gall of the king cobra, believed to have medicinal properties.
  • 肉可治疯 - Suggests that consuming snake meat could cure the disease, reflecting a folk belief in the medicinal value of certain animals.
  • 渐可 - Means 'gradually improved' or 'showed signs of recovery.'
  • 百日平复 - Means 'fully recovered within a hundred days.'
  • 商州 - Refers to a region in Shaanxi Province, China.
  • 茅舎 - Means 'hut made of straw,' indicating a simple, rural dwelling.
  • 乌蛇 - Refers to a black snake, likely a water snake or another species.
  • 罂 - Refers to a large earthenware jar, often used for storing liquids.
  • 踣面 - Means 'face down' or 'lying face down.'
  • 目直视不语 - Means 'staring straight ahead without speaking.'
  • 口中涎落 - Means 'saliva dripping from the mouth.'
  • 给使 - Refers to attendants or servants.
  • 长参典 - Refers to a senior official or attendant.
  • 怪其久 - Means 'wondered why it took so long.'
  • 私往候之 - Means 'went privately to check on him.'
  • 直入景运门 - Means 'entered the Jingyun Gate directly.'
  • 光政门 - Refers to a gate in the palace complex.
  • 太平公主 - Refers to Princess Taiping, a powerful figure during the Tang Dynasty.
  • 凤阁侍郎 - Refers to a high-ranking official in the Tang Dynasty government.
  • 周允元 - The name of the official mentioned in the text.
  • 朝罢入阁 - Means 'after court, entered the government office.'
  • 医人 - Refers to a doctor or physician.
  • 锦被覆之 - Means 'covered with a brocade quilt.'
  • 舁送宅 - Means 'carried to his residence.'
  • 夜半而卒 - Means 'died in the middle of the night.'
  • 悼之 - Means 'to mourn or lament.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 大疯 - In the context of the Tang Dynasty, '大疯' likely refers to leprosy or another severe skin disease.
  • 蚺蛇胆 - The belief in the medicinal properties of snake bile was common in traditional Chinese medicine.
  • 太平公主 - Princess Taiping was a powerful and influential figure during the reign of Emperor Zhongzong of Tang.
  • 凤阁侍郎 - This was a high-ranking position in the Tang Dynasty government, equivalent to a modern minister.
  • 周允元 - A historical figure who served as a high-ranking official during the Tang Dynasty.
  • 景运门 - One of the gates in the Tang Dynasty palace complex, symbolizing the imperial authority.
  • 锦被 - A luxurious quilt, indicating the high status of the person being treated.
  • 则天 - Refers to Empress Wu Zetian, the only female emperor in Chinese history.

Proper Nouns

  • 泉州 - Refers to Quanzhou, a city in Fujian Province, China.
  • 卢元钦 - The name of the person who recovered from a severe skin disease.
  • 商州 - Refers to Shangzhou, a region in Shaanxi Province, China.
  • 太平公主 - Refers to Princess Taiping, a powerful figure during the Tang Dynasty.
  • 凤阁侍郎 - Refers to a high-ranking official in the Tang Dynasty government.
  • 周允元 - The name of the official mentioned in the text.
  • 则天 - Refers to Empress Wu Zetian, the only female emperor in Chinese history.
  • 光政门 - One of the gates in the Tang Dynasty palace complex.
  • 景运门 - One of the gates in the Tang Dynasty palace complex.
  • 锦被 - A luxurious quilt, indicating the high status of the person being treated.

[6]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 久视年中 - Refers to the year 700 AD during the reign of Emperor Zhongzong of Tang.
  • 襄州人 - Refers to someone from Xiangzhou (modern-day Xiangyang, Hubei).
  • 于防州汶山观佣力 - Means working at the Wenshan Temple in Fangzhou (modern-day Chengdu, Sichuan).
  • 昼梦见天尊云 - Describes a dream where the person sees a deity (天尊, usually referring to the Jade Emperor or a high-ranking Taoist deity) speaking.
  • 我堂舎破坏汝为我脩造 - The deity asks the person to repair its temple.
  • 遣汝能医一切病 - The deity grants the person the ability to cure all diseases.
  • 悟而説之 - The person wakes up and realizes what happened in the dream.
  • 试疗无不愈者 - The person tests the ability and finds that it works.
  • 贑县里正背有肿大如拳 - Describes a village headman from Gan County with a swelling as big as a fist on his back.
  • 亮以刀割之数日平复 - The person cuts the swelling, and it heals within a few days.
  • 疗病日获十千 - The person earns ten thousand coins per day by treating patients.
  • 造天尊堂成疗病渐无效 - After building the temple for the deity, the person's healing ability gradually diminishes.
  • 如意年中 - Refers to the year 696 AD during the reign of Emperor Zhongzong of Tang.
  • 洛州人赵景病卒五日而苏 - A person from Luozhou (modern-day Luoyang, Henan) dies and comes back to life after five days.
  • 云见一僧与一木长尺余 - The person claims to have seen a monk giving him a piece of wood about a foot long.
  • 教曰人有病者汝以此木拄之即愈 - The monk instructs the person to use the wood to treat illnesses.
  • 景后见机上尺乃是僧所与者 - The person later finds the wood on a table.
  • 试将疗病拄之立差 - The person tests the wood, and it immediately cures the patient.
  • 门庭每日数百人 - The person's home becomes crowded with hundreds of patients every day.
  • 御史马知己以其聚众追之 - The censor Ma Jizhi investigates the person for gathering a crowd.
  • 禁左台病者满于台门 - The person's patients fill the left office of the censor's headquarters.
  • 则天闻之追入内宫 - Empress Wu Zetian hears about the person and summons him to the palace.
  • 人病拄之即愈 - The person cures the emperor's patients with the wood.
  • 放出任救百姓病 - The emperor allows the person to treat the common people.
  • 数月以后得钱七百余贯 - After several months, the person earns more than seven hundred strings of cash.
  • 后渐无验遂絶 - The wood eventually loses its effectiveness and disappears.
  • 洛州有人患应语病 - A person from Luozhou suffers from a condition where his throat responds to speech.
  • 语即喉中应之 - When the person speaks, his throat responds.
  • 以问善医张文仲 - The person seeks help from the skilled doctor Zhang Wenzhong.
  • 经夜思之乃得一法 - Zhang Wenzhong thinks about it overnight and comes up with a method.
  • 即取本草令读之皆应至其所畏者即不言 - He reads the *Bencao* (Herbal Medicine) aloud, and the person's throat stops responding when it reaches a particular herb.
  • 仲乃録取药合和为丸服之应时而愈 - Zhang Wenzhong collects the herbs, combines them into pills, and the person takes them, and the condition is cured immediately.
  • 一云问医苏澄云 - Another account says the person consulted the doctor Su Cheng.
  • 郝公景于泰山采药经市过有见者怪羣见公景皆走避之 - Hao Gongjing, while collecting herbs on Mount Tai, encounters a ghost who sees him and runs away.
  • 遂取药和为杀丸 - Hao makes a pill to kill ghosts.
  • 有病患者服之差 - The pill cures the patient's illness.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 久视年 - Refers to the year 700 AD during the reign of Emperor Zhongzong of Tang.
  • 如意年 - Refers to the year 696 AD during the reign of Emperor Zhongzong of Tang.
  • 天尊 - Refers to a high-ranking Taoist deity, often the Jade Emperor.
  • 天尊堂 - A temple dedicated to the deity.
  • 御史 - A high-ranking official responsible for supervision and investigation.
  • 左台 - The left office of the censor's headquarters.
  • 则天 - Empress Wu Zetian, the only female emperor in Chinese history.
  • 本草 - The *Bencao* (Herbal Medicine), a classical text on traditional Chinese medicine.
  • 泰山 - Mount Tai, one of the Five Great Mountains in China, known for its spiritual and cultural significance.

Proper Nouns

  • 久视年 - 700 AD
  • 如意年 - 696 AD
  • 襄州 - Modern-day Xiangyang, Hubei
  • 防州 - Modern-day Chengdu, Sichuan
  • 汶山观 - Wenshan Temple
  • 天尊 - A high-ranking Taoist deity
  • 天尊堂 - Temple dedicated to the deity
  • 贑县 - Modern-day Gan County, Jiangxi
  • 洛州 - Modern-day Luoyang, Henan
  • 赵景 - Zhao Xuanjing
  • 御史马知己 - Censor Ma Jizhi
  • 则天 - Empress Wu Zetian
  • 张文仲 - Zhang Wenzhong
  • 苏澄 - Su Cheng
  • 郝公景 - Hao Gongjing
  • 泰山 - Mount Tai

[7]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 定州人崔務墜馬折足 - A person from Dingzhou, Cui Wu, fell off a horse and broke his foot.
  • 醫令取銅末和酒服之遂痊平 - The doctor ordered him to take copper powder mixed with wine, and he was completely cured.
  • 及亡後十余年改視其胫骨折處有銅末束之 - After his death, more than ten years later, when he was reburied, it was seen that his fractured shinbone was bound with copper powder.
  • 岭南风俗多为毒药令奴食冶葛死埋之土中蕈生正当腹上食之立死 - In the Lingnan region, it is a custom to make poison, give it to slaves to eat, and bury them in the ground. Mushrooms grow directly over their stomachs, and eating them causes immediate death.
  • 惟陈怀卿家药能解之或以涂马鞭头控上拂着手即毒拭着口即死 - Only Chen Huaiqing's family medicine can cure it; if it is applied to the head of a horsewhip and waved around, it is toxic, and touching it or wiping it on the mouth will cause death.
  • 赵延禧云遭恶蛇虺所螫处贴之艾炷其上灸之立差不然即死凡蛇囓即当囓处灸之引去毒气即止 - Zhao Yanxi said that for a bite from a venomous snake, apply moxa to the spot and burn it, which will immediately heal the wound; otherwise, it will be fatal. For any snake bite, burn the spot where it bit, and the poison will be drawn away.
  • 冶葛食之立死有冶葛处即有白藤花能解冶葛毒鸩鸟食水之处即有犀牛不濯角其水物食之必死为鸩食蛇之故 - Eating wild gourd causes immediate death; where wild gourd grows, there is white morning glory that can cure its poison. Where the kingfisher drinks, there is a rhinoceros that does not wash its horn; anything that drinks that water will die because the kingfisher eats snakes.
  • 医书言虎中药箭食清泥野猪中药箭豗荠苨而食雉被鹰伤以地黄叶帖之又礜石可以害鼠张鷟曾试之鼠中毒如醉亦不识人犹知取泥汁饮之须臾平复鸟兽虫物解毒何况人乎被蠭齧者以甲虫末傅之被马咬者以烧鞭鞘灰涂之葢取其相服也蜘蛛齧者雄黄末傅之筋断须续者取旋复根绞取汁以筋相对以汁涂而封之即相续如故蜀儿奴逃走多刻筋以此续之百不失一 - Medical books say that a tiger bitten by a poisoned arrow eats clear mud; a wild boar bitten by a poisoned arrow eats wild ginger and wild ginger; a pheasant wounded by an eagle has its wound treated with rehmannia leaves. Orpiment can harm mice; Zhang Zhuo once tried it, and the mice became intoxicated, not recognizing people, but still knew to drink muddy water to recover. Birds, beasts, insects, and other creatures can be cured of poison; how much more so humans? For bee stings, apply beetle powder; for horse bites, apply burned horsewhip ash. It is taken from their mutual compatibility. For spider bites, apply realgar powder; for broken tendons, take the root of *Inula japonica*, squeeze out the juice, place the tendons opposite each other, and apply the juice and seal them; they will reconnect as before. In Sichuan, children who run away often cut their tendons and use this method to reconnect them, with no failures.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • This sentence describes a sequence of events: falling off a horse, breaking a foot, receiving a treatment, and being cured.
  • This sentence describes a posthumous observation, noting the presence of copper powder on a fractured shinbone.
  • This sentence describes a cultural practice involving poisoning and the growth of mushrooms over the buried bodies.
  • This sentence details the effects of the poison based on where it grows, using a series of conditional statements.
  • This sentence describes a specific antidote and its method of use, emphasizing its effectiveness and the danger of improper application.
  • This sentence describes a traditional remedy for snake bites, emphasizing the importance of immediate treatment.
  • This sentence describes a complex interplay of plants, animals, and poisons, using a series of conditional statements.
  • This sentence describes various traditional remedies for different ailments and injuries, using a series of conditional statements and examples.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 天后中 - Period during the reign of Wu Zetian, the only female emperor in Chinese history.
  • 契丹李尽忠万荣之破营府也 - Describes the defeat of the Tang Dynasty's military camp by the Khitan leader Li Jinzhong and his ally Wan Rong.
  • 地牢囚汉俘数百人 - Refers to the imprisonment of hundreds of Han captives in underground prisons.
  • 守囚霫等 - Refers to the guards or captors of the prisoners.
  • 绐之曰 - Means 'deceived them by saying,' indicating a false statement made to mislead.
  • 家口饥寒不能存活 - Means 'our families are starving and freezing, unable to survive,' a fabricated excuse to manipulate the prisoners.
  • 求待国家兵到吾等即降 - Means 'we will wait for the arrival of the national army and then surrender,' another deceptive statement.
  • 引出安慰曰 - Means 'led them out to comfort them by saying,' indicating a false promise to ease their minds.
  • 吾此无饮食养汝 - Means 'I have no food to feed you,' a lie to manipulate the prisoners.
  • 又不忍杀汝 - Means 'but I cannot bear to kill you,' another false statement.
  • 总放归 - Means 'let them all go,' indicating the release of the prisoners.
  • 逗遛兵士 - Means 'delaying the soldiers,' indicating that the prisoners were used to delay the Tang army.
  • 争欲先入 - Means 'racing to enter first,' indicating the eagerness of the soldiers to reach a destination.
  • 黄麞峪 - Refers to Huangzhang Valley, a location where the Tang army was ambushed.
  • 被贼设伏横截军将被索防之生擒节等死者填山谷罕有一遗 - Describes the ambush and capture of the Tang generals, with many soldiers dying in the valley.
  • 景龙四年 - Refers to the year 708 AD, during the reign of Emperor Zhongzong of Tang.
  • 洛州凌空观失火 - Refers to a fire at Lingkong Temple in Luoyang.
  • 万物并尽 - Means 'all things were destroyed,' indicating the complete destruction of the temple.
  • 惟有一真人岿然独存 - Means 'only one statue of a Taoist deity remained standing,' indicating the survival of a single statue amidst the destruction.
  • 乃泥塑为之后改为圣真观 - Means 'it was then made into a clay statue, and the temple was renamed Shengzhen Temple,' indicating the transformation of the temple after the fire.
  • 西京朝堂北头有大槐树 - Refers to a large locust tree in front of the northern gate of the imperial court in Chang'an.
  • 隋曰唐兴材门首 - Means 'during the Sui Dynasty, it was called the Tangxing Gate,' indicating the historical name of the gate.
  • 文皇帝移长安城 - Refers to Emperor Wen of Sui, who moved the capital from Chang'an to Luoyang.
  • 将作大匠高颎常坐此树下检校 - Means 'the chief architect Gao Jing often sat under this tree to inspect,' indicating Gao Jing's habit of inspecting the tree.
  • 栽树行不正欲去之 - Means 'the tree was planted crookedly, and there was a desire to remove it,' indicating the tree's poor condition.
  • 帝曰颎坐此树下不须杀之 - Means 'the emperor said, 'Gao Jing sat under this tree, so do not cut it down,' indicating the emperor's decision to preserve the tree.
  • 至今先天一百三十年其树尚在柯叶森竦株根盘礴与诸树不同 - Means 'until today, one hundred and thirty years after the beginning of the Tianbao era, the tree still stands with its branches and leaves towering and its roots sprawling, different from other trees,' indicating the tree's longevity and unique appearance.
  • 承天门正当唐兴材门首今唐家居焉 - Means 'Chenting Gate is directly opposite the Tangxing Gate, and now the Tang family resides there,' indicating the current location of the Tang family.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 知海运失船极多除名为民遂辽东效力 - Knowing about many ship losses, being demoted to commoner status, and subsequently serving in Liaodong to redeem oneself.
  • 遇病卧平壤城下搴幕看兵士攻城 - Encountering illness while lying outside the city walls of Pingyang, pulling aside the curtain to watch soldiers attacking the city.
  • 直来前头背坐叱之不去仍恶骂曰 - Coming straight up to the front, sitting with one's back, scolding and not leaving, still cursing and saying.
  • 你欲看我亦欲看何预汝事 - You want to look, I want to look, what business is it of yours? A defensive and dismissive response.
  • 不肯去须臾城头放箭正中心而死 - Refused to leave, shortly after an arrow was shot from the city wall, hitting him directly in the heart and killing him.
  • 微此兵仁轨防为矢所中 - Without this soldier, Governor Ren Gui would have been hit by an arrow, indicating a narrow escape.
  • 任之选与张说同时应举后说为中书令之选竟不及第 - Ren Zhi was selected to take the imperial examinations at the same time as Zhang Yue, after Zhang Yue became a Grand Secretary, Ren Zhi was never selected.
  • 来谒张公公遗绢一束以充粮用 - Came to visit Zhang Gong, who gave him a bundle of silk to use for food expenses.
  • 将归至舎不经一两月疾大作 - On the way back to his residence, after not even one or two months, his illness worsened.
  • 将绢市药绢尽疾自损 - Used the silk to buy medicine, and when the silk ran out, his illness worsened on its own.
  • 何薄命之甚也 - How unfortunate is my fate! A lamentation about one's misfortune.
  • 杭州刺史裴有敞疾甚令钱塘县主簿夏荣看之 - Governor Pei Youchang of Hangzhou fell gravely ill, ordered the County Magistrate of Qiantang, Xia Rong, to visit him.
  • 荣曰使君百无一虑夫人早须崇福以禳之 - Xia Rong said, 'Sir, there is nothing to worry about; your wife should build merit early to ward off misfortune.'
  • 崔夫人曰禳须何物 - Lady Cui said, 'What is needed to ward off misfortune?'
  • 荣曰使君娶二姬以压之出三年则危过矣 - Xia Rong said, 'Sir, you should marry two concubines to suppress it; if you do not do so within three years, the danger will be even greater.'
  • 夫人怒曰此獠狂语儿在身无病 - Lady Cui angrily said, 'This barbarian is crazy; my son is healthy.'
  • 荣退曰夫人不信荣不敢言使君命合有三妇不更娶于夫人不祥 - Xia Rong stepped back and said, 'Lady, if you do not believe me, I dare not speak further; Sir's fate is destined to have three wives; if he does not marry again, it will be a bad omen.'
  • 夫人曰乍可死此事不相当也 - Lady Cui said, 'It is better to die than to be involved in such matters.'
  • 其年夫人暴亡 - That year, Lady Cui suddenly died.
  • 敞更娶二姓 - Pei Youchang remarried two more women.
  • 荣言信矣 - Xia Rong's words proved true.
  • 广平王诛逆韦崔日用将兵杜曲诛诸韦略尽 - Prince Guangping killed the traitorous Wei Cui, and General Du Qu led the troops to almost completely eliminate the Wei clan.
  • 绷子中婴孩亦捏杀之 - Even infants in their cradles were strangled to death.
  • 诸杜滥及者非一 - Many others were also wrongfully killed.
  • 浮休子曰此逆韦之罪疎族何辜亦如冉闵杀畨高鼻者横死董卓诛阉人无须者枉戮死生命也 - Fuhuizi said, 'This is the crime of the traitorous Wei; why should distant relatives suffer? It is like Ran Min killing foreigners with high noses, or Dong Zhuo executing eunuchs without beards, all of which are unjust deaths; such is the will of fate.'

  • 明公看祎何當遷 - Zhang Renyi asked Shen Junliang when he would be promoted.
  • 台郎坐不暖席 - A metaphorical expression meaning that Zhang Renyi had not been in his position long enough to get comfortable.
  • 俄而祎如厠 - Zhang Renyi went to the bathroom.
  • 張員外總十余日活 - Shen Junliang said Zhang Renyi would live for more than ten days.
  • 后七日而祎卒 - Zhang Renyi died seven days later.
  • 防州司士 - A local official in charge of military affairs.
  • 劉知元 - A specific official, Liu Zhimou.
  • 攝判司倉 - Liu Zhimou temporarily took over the position of managing the granary.
  • 大酺 - A grand feast or celebration.
  • 司马杨舜臣 - A military official named Yang Shunchen.
  • 買肉必須胎肥脆可食 - Liu Zhimou said that meat must be from pregnant animals to be tender and tasty.
  • 余瘦不堪 - Meat from non-pregnant animals is not suitable.
  • 防取懷孕牛犊及猪羊驴等殺之 - Liu Zhimou ordered the killing of pregnant cattle, pigs, sheep, and donkeys.
  • 其胎仍動良久乃絶 - The fetuses continued to move for a long time before dying.
  • 無何舜臣一奴無病而死 - Yang Shunchen's servant died without any apparent illness.
  • 心上乃暖七日而蘇 - The servant's heart warmed up, and he revived after seven days.
  • 見王訴云懷胎五个月扛殺母子 - The water buffalo and its offspring claimed that they were killed because the mother was five months pregnant.
  • 須臾又見猪羊驴等皆領子來訴 - Soon after, the servant saw pigs, sheep, and donkeys with their offspring coming to complain.
  • 見劉司士答欵引楊司马处分如此 - Liu Zhimou admitted to the servant that he had followed Yang Shunchen's orders.
  • 居三日而知元卒亡 - Liu Zhimou died three days later.
  • 又五日而舜臣死 - Yang Shunchen died five days after Liu Zhimou.
  • 率更令 - A local official in charge of timekeeping.
  • 张文成 - A specific official, Zhang Wencheng.
  • 枭晨鸣于庭树 - An owl called out early in the morning from a tree in the courtyard.
  • 其妻以為不祥 - Zhang Wencheng's wife thought it was an omen of bad luck.
  • 連唾之 - She spat at the owl.
  • 文成云急洒扫吾当改官 - Zhang Wencheng said that he should clean the house quickly because he was about to be promoted.
  • 言未畢贺客已在门矣 - Before he finished speaking, guests arrived to congratulate him.

  • 調露之後有鳥大如鳩色如烏鵲飛風聲千萬為隊時人謂之鵽雀亦名突厥雀若來突厥必至後至無差 - Describes a bird seen after the Tiaolu era, which was considered an omen of the arrival of the Turks.
  • 天授中則天好改新字又多忌諱有幽州人尋如意上封云國字中或或亂天象請囗中安武以鎮之則天大喜下制即依月余有上封者云武退在囗中與囚字無異不祥之甚則天愕然遽追制改令中為八方字後孝和即位果幽則天於上陽宮 - Describes Wu Zetian's interest in creating new characters and the subsequent changes in the character '囗' due to superstitions and omens.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 好食人肉 - Literal meaning 'likes to eat human flesh,' a euphemism for cannibalism.
  • 债主及奴诣临安于客舎遂饮之醉杀而脔之以水银和煎幷骨销尽 - Describes a violent sequence: creditor and slave arrived at Lin'an in an inn, drank with the victim, got him drunk, killed him, chopped him into pieces, cooked with mercury, and melted down to the bones.
  • 周岭南首领陈元光设客令一袍袴行酒光怒令曳出遂杀之须防烂煮以食客后呈其二手客惧攫喉而吐 - During the Zhou dynasty, the leader of Lingnan, Chen Yuanguang, set up a banquet and ordered a servant to serve wine, Chen became angry, ordered him to be dragged out and killed, his body was boiled until soft and served to guests, later his hands were shown, guests were terrified and choked and vomited.
  • 周瀛州刺史独孤庄酷虐有贼问不承庄引前曰若健儿一一具吐放汝遂还巾带贼并吐之诸官以为必放顷庄曰将我作具来乃一铁钩长丈余甚铦利以绳挂于树间谓贼曰汝不闻健儿钩下死今以胲钩之遣壮士掣其绳则钩出于脑矣谓司法曰此法何似答曰吊民伐罪深得其宜庄大笑后庄左降施州刺史染病惟忆人肉部下有奴婢死者遣人割肋下肉食之嵗余卒 - During the Zhou dynasty, the governor of Yingzhou, Dugu Zhuang, was cruel, when a bandit did not confess, he said if he were a soldier, he would confess everything and be released, he then returned his headband, the bandit confessed everything, all officials thought he would be released, but soon Dugu said bring me a tool, it was a long iron hook, very sharp, and he hung it on a tree, he told the bandit, you have never heard of soldiers dying under hooks, now I will hook you and send a strong man to pull the rope, and the hook will come out of your brain, he asked the judge, how is this method? The judge said it is appropriate to pacify the people and punish the guilty, Dugu laughed, later he was demoted to be the governor of Shizhou, he fell ill and only thought of human flesh, he had his dead slaves and servants' rib meat cut and eaten, he died after a year.
  • 周推事使索元礼时人号为索使讯囚作铁笼头【呼角反】其头仍加揳焉多至脑裂髓出又为凤晒翅狝猴鑚火等以椽闗手足而转之并斫骨至碎又悬囚于梁下以石缒头其酷法如此元礼外域人薛师之假父后坐贿流死岭南 - During the Zhou dynasty, the interrogator Suyanli was called the 'Suo' interrogator, he interrogated prisoners by making an iron cage for their heads, he also added wedges, often causing their brains to split and their marrow to come out, he also made phoenixes spread their wings, macaques drill fire, etc., he bound their hands and feet with wooden poles and turned them, and chopped their bones to pieces, he also hung prisoners under the beams and lowered them with stones, his cruel methods were like this, Suyanli was an outsider, he was the foster father of Xue Shizhi, later he was convicted of corruption and exiled to die in Lingnan.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 隋開皇中 - Refers to the reign of Emperor Wen of Sui during the Kaihuang era, a period of consolidation and reform.
  • 京兆韋衮有奴曰桃符 - A standard narrative structure: 'A man from Jingzhao named Wei Gou had a servant named Tao Fu.'
  • 每征討將行有膽力 - 'Whenever he was about to go on a campaign, he showed courage and strength.'
  • 乃放從良 - 'He then allowed him to become a free person (i.e., to be registered as a commoner).'
  • 黃防宰 - A likely misspelling or variant of 黃犢子 (huáng dú zǐ), which means 'yellow calf' or 'yellow ox calf.' This is a metaphor or nickname, possibly indicating a low social status or a name given to a servant.
  • 因問衮乞姓 - 'Because of this, he asked Gou to take his surname.'
  • 止從我姓為韋氏 - 'Just take my surname and be of the Wei family.'
  • 符叩頭曰不敢與郎君同姓 - 'Tao Fu knelt and said, 'I dare not share the same surname as your honorable master.''
  • 此有深意故至今為黃犢子 - 'This has a deep meaning, hence he is still called 'Yellow Calf.''
  • 則天后 - Refers to Wu Zetian, the only female emperor of China, known as the 'Later Emperor Wu' or 'Empress Wu.'
  • 兩趐俱折 - 'Both wings were broken,' a metaphor for the death or downfall of two sons.
  • 陛下二子庐陵相王也 - 'These are your two sons, the Prince of庐陵 and the Prince of Xiangwang.'
  • 兩趐全也 - 'Both wings are whole,' meaning the sons are safe or restored.
  • 武承嗣武三思連項皆赤 - 'Wu Chengsi and Wu Sanshi had red necks,' a metaphor for being close to power or being in favor with the empress.
  • 后契丹圍幽州 - 'Later, the Khitan surrounded Youzhou,' referring to a military threat.
  • 檄朝廷曰還我庐陵相王來 - 'They sent a proclamation to the court saying, 'Return our Prince of Xiangwang!''
  • 朕欲立太子何者為得 - 'I wish to name a crown prince; who is the most suitable?'
  • 取舍詳择斷在聖衷 - 'The choice between them should be made by the emperor's own judgment.'
  • 我自有聖子承嗣三思是何疥癣 - 'I have my own virtuous son; Chengsi and Sanshi are merely skin diseases.'
  • 掩耳而走 - 'Covered their ears and ran away,' a metaphor for ignoring or rejecting the suggestion.
  • 降敕追庐陵立為太子 - 'She issued an edict to summon the Prince of庐陵 and make him crown prince.'
  • 初募兵無有應者 - 'At first, when recruiting soldiers, no one responded.'
  • 聞太子行北印山頭皆兵滿無容人處 - 'When word spread that the太子 was coming, the northern hills were filled with soldiers, no room for people.'
  • 賊自退散 - 'The enemy withdrew on their own.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 隋開皇 - The Kaihuang era (581–600) of the Sui dynasty, known for its administrative reforms and unification of China.
  • 京兆 - The administrative region of Jingzhao, which was the area around present-day Xi'an, the capital of the Sui and Tang dynasties.
  • 韋衮 - A historical figure, a high-ranking official in the Sui court, holding the title of Left Guard Langzhong.
  • 桃符 - A common servant name, possibly symbolic or metaphorical.
  • 黃犢子 - A nickname or term of endearment, possibly reflecting the servant's low status or a name given by his master. The term may also have a symbolic or metaphorical meaning.
  • 韋庶人 - Refers to Empress Wu Zetian (Wu Zetian was originally a concubine of Emperor Gaozong, later becoming the only female emperor of China). The term '韋' may be a misinterpretation or symbolic reference, as Wu Zetian did not belong to the Wei clan. This may be a confusion in the text or an allegorical reference.
  • 則天后 - The title of Wu Zetian during her time as empress consort and later as emperor. '則天' means 'to follow heaven' or 'to govern in accordance with heaven,' a title she took after her ascension.
  • 契丹 - The Khitan people, a nomadic group that later founded the Liao dynasty.
  • 幽州 - A military and administrative region in northern China, roughly corresponding to modern-day Beijing.
  • 庐陵相王 - Refers to Li Xian, the Prince of庐陵, who later became Emperor Ruizong of Tang and was Wu Zetian’s son.
  • 武承嗣 - Wu Chengsi, Wu Zetian’s nephew, who was a powerful figure in the court and her political ally.
  • 武三思 - Wu Sanshi, Wu Zetian’s nephew, who was a powerful figure in the court and her political ally.
  • 太子 - Crown prince, the heir to the throne.
  • 北印山 - Likely a geographical reference, possibly a mountain or hill in the northern region of the empire, used to emphasize the sudden mobilization of forces in response to the prince's arrival.

  • 隋 - Sui dynasty
  • 開皇 - Kaihuang era of the Sui dynasty
  • 京兆 - Jingzhao

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The phrase '生龙肝' is ambiguous. It could mean 'fresh dragon liver' or 'live dragon liver.'
  • The phrase '乘风放之' is ambiguous. It could mean 'sent with the wind' or 'released with the wind.'
  • The phrase '须臾有龙下入瓮水中' is ambiguous. It could mean 'soon a dragon descended into the water in the jar' or 'soon a dragon entered the jar containing water.'
  • The phrase '剔取食之而差' is ambiguous. It could mean 'the liver was extracted and eaten, and the illness was cured' or 'the liver was taken out and eaten, and the illness was cured.'
  • The phrase '大帝盛夏须雪及枇杷龙眼' is ambiguous. It could mean 'the emperor needed snow and loquat and longan fruits during the hot summer' or 'the emperor needed snow and loquat and longan fruits in the summer.'
  • The phrase '俨坐顷间往阴山取雪岭南取果子' is ambiguous. It could mean 'Ming Chongyan sat for a moment and went to the Yin Mountains to fetch snow and to the south to fetch fruits' or 'Ming Chongyan sat for a moment and went to the Yin Mountains to fetch snow and to the south to fetch fruits.'
  • The phrase '四月未熟上思之' is ambiguous. It could mean 'In April, the fruits were not yet ripe, but the emperor thought about them' or 'In April, the fruits were not yet ripe, and the emperor thought about them.'
  • The phrase '俨索百钱将去' is ambiguous. It could mean 'Ming Chongyan asked for a hundred coins to leave' or 'Ming Chongyan asked for a hundred coins and left.'
  • The phrase '得一大果云缑氏老人园内得之' is ambiguous. It could mean 'He obtained a large fruit, saying it was from the garden of an old man in Gou City' or 'He obtained a large fruit, saying it came from the garden of an old man in Gou City.'
  • The phrase '敕求贼甚急竟无踪绪' is ambiguous. It could mean 'The imperial decree sought the thief urgently, but there was no trace of him' or 'The imperial decree sought the thief urgently, and there was no trace of him.'
  • The phrase '或以为俨役鬼劳苦被鬼杀之' is ambiguous. It could mean 'Some believed that Ming Chongyan, who had been working with spirits, was killed by a ghost due to exhaustion' or 'Some believed that Ming Chongyan, who had been working with spirits, was killed by a ghost because of exhaustion.'
  • The phrase '攻乎异端斯害也已' is ambiguous. It could mean 'Attacking heterodox ideas is itself a harm' or 'Attacking heterodox ideas is itself a danger.'

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 善書瘦怯于父 - Literally means 'good at writing but more slender and timid than his father.' This phrase describes the son's writing style and demeanor compared to his father's.
  • 必以牙犀角为笔管 - Means 'he must use ivory and rhinoceros horn as pen tubes,' indicating the high-quality materials he uses for writing.
  • 狸毛为心覆以秋兎毫 - Means 'use cat hair as the core and cover it with autumn rabbit hair,' describing the composition of the brush he uses.
  • 松烟为墨末以麝香 - Means 'use pine soot as ink and add musk,' indicating the quality and fragrance of the ink he uses.
  • 纸必须坚薄白滑者乃书之 - Means 'the paper must be firm, thin, white, and smooth before he writes,' emphasizing the quality of the paper he prefers.
  • 盖自重其书 - Means 'this shows his high regard for his own writing,' indicating his pride in his work.
  • 薛纯陀亦效欧阳草伤于肥钝亦通之亚也 - Means 'Xue Chuntuo also imitated Ouyang's cursive script, but it was too heavy and blunt, making it inferior to Ouyang's,' comparing the styles of the two writers.
  • 少时病忽亡见衙府如平生时不知其死 - Means 'he fell ill and suddenly died, seeing the official palace as he had in life, unaware of his death,' describing a ghostly experience.
  • 因何得来具报之乃知是防途吏为检寻 - Means 'why did you come here? Report it, and you will know that a boundary official is checking,' explaining the context of the ghost's appearance.
  • 君平生无修福处何以得还 - Means 'you have not done any good deeds in life, so how did you return?' questioning the ghost's ability to return.
  • 吏问欲知官乎曰甚要遂以簿示之云孟知俭合运出身为曹州参军转邓州司佥即掩却不许看遂至荒榛入一黑坑遂活不知运是何事 - Means 'the official asked if he wanted to know his position, saying it was very important, and showed him a register, saying 'Meng Zjian is destined to be a magistrate in Caozhou, then transfer to a clerk in Dengzhou. He immediately covered it and did not allow him to see it, so he went to a desolate thicket and entered a black pit, and then he lived. He did not know what his destiny was,' describing the ghost's fate.
  • 寻有敕募运粮因放选授曹州参军乃悟曰此州吾不见小书耳 - Means 'soon there was an imperial decree to recruit for transporting grain, and he was appointed as a magistrate in Caozhou. He realized, 'This state I did not see a small book,' meaning he recognized the state mentioned in the register,' indicating his realization.
  • 满授邓州司佥去任又选唱晋州判司未过而卒 - Means 'he was fully appointed as a clerk in Dengzhou, and after leaving his post, he was selected and appointed as a judge in Jinzhou, but he died before he could take up the position,' describing his final career move.

  • 秋分后无计可作 - After the autumn equinox, there was nothing to do, suggesting a period of inactivity.
  • 圜狱外罗墙角边作圣人迹 - Traces of a sage were made at the corner of the prison wall, indicating a deliberate act to create a sign.
  • 长五尺 - Five feet long, describing the size of the trace.
  • 至夜半三百人一时大呌 - At midnight, all three hundred prisoners suddenly cried out, indicating a collective reaction.
  • 内使推问 - The eunuchs investigated, referring to the palace officials who conducted the investigation.
  • 昨夜有圣人见身长三丈面作金色云 - Last night, a sage appeared, three zhang tall, with a golden face, describing the appearance of the sage.
  • 汝等并寃枉不须怕惧 - You all are wronged, do not be afraid, indicating a message of reassurance.
  • 天子万年即有恩赦放汝 - The emperor will live for a long time and will grant amnesty to you, promising future relief.
  • 把火照之见有巨迹 - When they lit a fire to look, they saw a large trace, indicating the discovery of the sign.

  • 白铁余者延州羁胡也 - Bai Tieyu was a captive Turk from Yan Prefecture, providing background on Bai Tieyu.
  • 左道惑众 - He used false doctrines to deceive the people, indicating his fraudulent practices.
  • 经数年草生其上 - After several years, grass grew on top of it, indicating the passage of time.
  • 绐乡人曰吾昨夜山下过毎见佛光大设斋卜吉日以出圣佛 - He told the villagers that last night he passed by the mountain and saw the Buddha's light. He set up a grand feast and chose a lucky day to reveal the holy Buddha, describing his deception.
  • 及期集数百人命于非所藏处斸不得 - On the appointed day, he gathered hundreds of people and ordered them to dig at the wrong place, but they could not find it, indicating the failure of the search.
  • 乃劝曰诸公不至诚布施佛不可见 - Then he encouraged them, saying that if they did not sincerely offer alms, the Buddha would not appear, manipulating the crowd.
  • 由是男女争布施者百余万 - As a result, men and women competed to offer alms, totaling more than a million, indicating the extent of the deception.
  • 更于埋处斸之得金铜像 - They dug at the burial site and found the gold and bronze statue, revealing the truth.
  • 乡人以圣逺近传之莫能见 - The villagers spread the news far and wide, but no one could see the saint, indicating the widespread belief.
  • 乃宣言曰见圣佛者百病即愈 - He then declared that anyone who saw the saint would be cured of all illnesses, using the statue to gain more followers.
  • 左侧数百里老小士女皆就之 - Within several hundred miles, old and young, men and women, all flocked to him, indicating the widespread influence.
  • 乃以绯紫红黄绫为袋数十重盛像 - He then placed the statue in a bag made of red, purple, red, and yellow silk, with dozens of layers, describing the elaborate preparation.
  • 人聚观者去一重一回布施千端乃见像 - People gathered to watch, and for each layer removed, they offered alms, totaling several thousand times, and then they saw the statue, indicating the ritual.
  • 如此狡伪一二年 - In this way, he was deceitful for one or two years, indicating the duration of his fraud.

  • 命将军程务挺斩之 - The general Cheng Wuteng was ordered to kill him, indicating the military action taken against him.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • 则天好祯祥拾遗朱前疑说梦云则天发白更黑齿落更生即授都官郎中司刑事 must be read as a single narrative, where Empress Wu experiences a miraculous event and is subsequently appointed to a high-ranking position.
  • 囚三百余人秋分后无计可作乃于圜狱外罗墙角边作圣人迹长五尺至夜半三百人一时大呌内使推问云昨夜有圣人见身长三丈面作金色云汝等并寃枉不须怕惧天子万年即有恩赦放汝把火照之见有巨迹即大赦天下改为大足元年 must be read as a series of events leading to a general amnesty and a change in the year.
  • 白铁余者延州羁胡也左道惑众先于深山中埋一金铜像于柏树之下经数年草生其上绐乡人曰吾昨夜山下过毎见佛光大设斋卜吉日以出圣佛及期集数百人命于非所藏处斸不得乃劝曰诸公不至诚布施佛不可见由是男女争布施者百余万更于埋处斸之得金铜像乡人以圣逺近传之莫能见乃宣言曰见圣佛者百病即愈左侧数百里老小士女皆就之乃以绯紫红黄绫为袋数十重盛像人聚观者去一重一回布施千端乃见像如此狡伪一二年乡人归伏遂作乱自号光王署置官职杀长吏数年为患 must be read as a series of events leading to the discovery of the statue and the subsequent actions taken by the villagers.

  • 仍潜入伏内食侍官饭晚出外腹痛犹诈云太子赐予食太多须臾霍出卫士所食米饭黄臭并虀菜狼籍 - Means 'he still secretly entered the palace to eat the food of the servants, leaving in the evening with an excuse of stomach pain, claiming the prince had given him too much fruit, and soon after, he expelled the soldiers, the rice was yellow and smelly, and the vegetables were scattered'.
  • 凡是小人得宠多为此状也 - Means 'this is the usual state of affairs for those who gain favor through deceit'.
  • 春官尚书阎知微和黙啜司賔丞田归道副焉 - Means 'Spring Official and Shangshu Yin Zhiwei and Deputy Guest Censor Tian Guaidao accompanied him'.
  • 至牙帐下知微舞蹈宛转抱黙啜鞾而鼻臭之 - Means 'when they arrived at the tent, Yin Zhiwei danced and twisted, embracing the boots of Moche and smelling them'.
  • 田归道长揖不拜 - Means 'Tian Guaidao bowed deeply but did not kowtow'.
  • 黙啜大怒倒悬之经一宿 - Means 'Moche became extremely angry and had him suspended upside down for an entire night'.
  • 明日将杀元珍谏大国和亲使若杀之不祥乃放之 - Means 'the next day, he intended to kill Yuan Zhen, who advised against killing the envoy of a great country, considering it inauspicious, so he released him'.
  • 后与知微争于殿庭言黙啜必不和知微坚执以为和黙啜果反陷赵定天后乃诛知微九族拜归道夏官侍郎 - Means 'later, they argued in the palace, saying Moche would not agree to peace, but Yin Zhiwei insisted that he would, and indeed, Moche rebelled, capturing Zhao and Ding. The empress regnant then executed Yin Zhiwei's entire family and promoted Tian Guaidao to the position of Shangshu in the Department of Works'.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The sentence '贪汚有一里正死范令门内一人为里正造像各出钱一贯范自纳之谓曰里正有过罪先须急捄范先造得一像且以与之结钱二千百【阙】五寸半' is complex and elliptical, describing a series of events involving the magistrate's corruption and the creation of a statue.
  • The sentence '益州新昌县令夏侯彪之初下车问里正曰鸡卵一钱几颗曰三颗彪之乃遣取十千钱买三万颗谓里正曰未须要且寄母鸡抱之遂成三万头鸡经数月长成令县吏与我卖一鸡三十钱半年之间成三十万' describes the magistrate's cunning scheme to acquire chickens by buying eggs.
  • The sentence '汴州刺史王志愔饮食精细对賔下脱粟饭商客有一骡日行三百里曽三十年不卖市人报价云十四千愔曰四千金少更増一千又令买单丝罗疋至三千愔问用几两丝对曰五两愔令竖子取五两丝来每两别与十钱手功之直' describes the magistrate's luxurious lifestyle and his dealings with a merchant.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 宗妹短丑武氏最长时号大歌县主 - Describes a short and unattractive sister of the Wu clan, who was the eldest and was called 'Dagē Xiànzhǔ' (大歌县主). '大歌' likely refers to a title or nickname.
  • 与则天并马行命元一咏曰 - Describes a situation where the sister and Wu Zetian (武则天) were riding together, and Wu Zetian ordered Yuan Yi (元一) to compose a poem.
  • 马带桃花锦裙拖緑草罗定知纱帽底形容似大歌 - A poem composed by Yuan Yi, describing the sister's appearance. '马带桃花锦裙拖緑草罗定知纱帽底形容似大歌' can be interpreted as 'The horse wears a peach blossom brocade skirt, dragging green grass, and the robe clearly shows the hat, resembling Dagē.'
  • 则天大笑县主极慙 - Wu Zetian laughed, and the sister felt extremely ashamed.
  • 纳言娄师德长大而黒一足蹇 - Describes a tall and dark official named Lou Shide (娄师德) who had a limp in one foot.
  • 元一目为行辙方相亦号为卫灵公 - Yuan Yi nicknamed Lou Shide 'Xingzhe Fangxiang' (行辙方相) and also called him 'Wei Linggong' (卫灵公). '方相' refers to a ritual figure, and '卫灵公' is a historical figure.
  • 天官侍郎吉顼长大好昻头行视髙而望逺目为望栁骆驼 - Describes a tall official named Ji Xu (吉顼) who liked to look up and far, and was nicknamed 'Wangliu Luotuo' (望栁骆驼).
  • 殿中侍御史元本竦体伛身黑而且瘦目为领南考典 - Describes a short and thin official named Yuan Ben (元本) who was nicknamed 'Lingnan Kaodian' (领南考典).
  • 驾部郎中朱前疑粗黑肥短身体垢腻目为光禄掌膳 - Describes a short and fat official named Zhu Qianyi (朱前疑) who was nicknamed 'Guanglu Zhaishan' (光禄掌膳).
  • 东方虬身长衫短骨面麤眉目为外军校尉 - Describes a tall official named Dongfang Qiu (东方虬) who was nicknamed 'Waijun Xiaowei' (外军校尉).
  • 唐波若矮短目为鬰屈蜀马目 - Describes a short official named Tang Bo'uo (唐波若) who was nicknamed 'Yuqu Shuma' (鬰屈蜀马).
  • 李昭德卒【子锐反】岁猢狲修文学士 - Describes a scholar named Li Zhaode (李昭德) who died, and was nicknamed 'Suihusun Xiuwen' (岁猢狲修文学士).
  • 马吉甫眇一目为端箭师 - Describes an official named Ma Jifu (马吉甫) who was one-eyed and was nicknamed 'Duanyanshi' (端箭师).
  • 郎中长孺子视望阳目为呷醋汉 - Describes an official named Zhang Ruzi (郎中长孺子) who was nicknamed 'Xiacu Han' (呷醋汉).
  • 泥水令苏徴举止轻薄目为失孔老鼠 - Describes an official named Su Zheng (苏徴) who was nicknamed 'Shikong Laozu' (失孔老鼠).
  • 周张元一腹麤而脚短项缩而眼跌吉顼目为逆流虾蟆 - Describes an official named Zhang Yuan Yi (张元一) who was nicknamed 'Niliu Xieha' (逆流虾蟆) by Ji Xu (吉顼).

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 弱冠 - Refers to a man who is 20 years old, having just undergone the capping ceremony, which marks the transition to adulthood.
  • 字曰 - Means 'to be given a courtesy name,' a practice in ancient China where a person was given a second name at adulthood.
  • 誠愚勞諸君制字損南容之身尚可豈可波及侍中也 - Translates roughly to 'Indeed, it is foolish to trouble you all with naming, which harms my son's dignity, but how much more so to affect the dignity of a chancellor?' The phrase '誠愚' (indeed foolish) and '損南容之身' (harm my son's dignity) are key idiomatic expressions.
  • 反語 - Means 'reversal of words,' often used to create a pun or a play on words.

  • 貌恭而性狠 - 外表恭敬而内心狠毒。
  • 智小而谋大 - 智谋小而野心大。
  • 趦趄狗盗 - 形容行为犹豫不决，像狗偷东西一样。
  • 突忽猪贪 - 形容行为突然，像猪一样贪婪。
  • 晨羊诱外 - 直译为“早晨的羊被引诱到外面”，可能指被外物诱惑。
  • 夜蛾覆烛 - 直译为“夜晚的蛾子扑向烛火”，比喻自取灭亡。
  • 头寄于项 - 直译为“头寄于颈项”，比喻地位不稳固。
  • 司农卿 - 古代官职，掌管国家财政。
  • 郑愔 - 人名，此处指郑愔。
  • 选部侍郎 - 古代官职，负责选拔官员。
  • 猖獗小子 - 形容人行为猖狂。
  • 狡猾庸人 - 形容人狡猾而平庸。
  • 浅学浮词 - 学识浅薄，言辞浮夸。
  • 狐蹲贵介 - 形容人像狐狸一样蹲在贵人的位置上。
  • 雉伏权门 - 形容人像野鸡一样伏在权贵的门前。
  • 前托俊臣后附张易 - 形容人前后依附不同的人。
  • 防痔安乐之庭 - 直译为“防止痔疮的安乐庭院”，可能指在安乐中防备祸患。
  • 鸋鳷栖于苇苕 - 鸋鳷是一种鸟，苇苕是芦苇的顶端，形容鸟栖息在芦苇顶端。
  • 魦鲿游于沸鼎 - 魦鲿是一种鱼，沸鼎是沸腾的锅，形容鱼在沸腾的锅中游动，比喻身处险境。
  • 无雅量 - 没有雅量，指缺乏宽广的胸怀。
  • 凡材 - 平凡的材料，指平庸的人。
  • 得死为幸 - 得到死亡是幸运的，指命运悲惨。
  • 谋反伏诛 - 因谋反而被处死。

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 渤海高嶷巨富 - Refers to a wealthy man named Gao Yi from Bohai.
  • 忽患月余日帖然而卒 - Describes an abrupt illness lasting over a month, followed by death.
  • 心上仍暖经日而苏 - The heart remained warm for several days before he revived.
  • 云有一白衣人眇目把牒防司讼杀其妻子 - He claimed that a blind man with a scroll was preventing his wife and children from being killed.
  • 君命未尽且放归 - The blind man said that his life was not yet finished and he would be returned.
  • 遂悟白衣人乃是家中老瞎麻鸡也 - He realized that the blind man was actually an old blind rooster from his home.
  • 令射杀魅遂绝 - He ordered the rooster to be shot, which ended the haunting.
  • 文明以后天下诸州进雌鸡变为雄者多或半已化半未化乃则天正位之兆 - After the reign of Emperor Zetian, many hens across various states turned into roosters, either partially or fully, which was seen as an omen of her rise to power.
  • 卫镐为县官下乡至里人王幸在家方假寐 - Wei Hao, a county official, visited a villager named Wang Xing who was just dozing off.
  • 梦一乌衣妇人引十数小儿着黄衣咸言乞命叩头再三 - In a dream, he saw a woman in black clothing leading several children in yellow clothing who were pleading for their lives.
  • 斯须又至镐甚恶其事遂催食欲前适所亲 - Shortly after, Wei Hao hurriedly ate and went to visit a friend.
  • 有报曰王幸在家穷无物设馔有一鸡见抱儿已得十余日将欲杀之 - He was informed that Wang Xing was poor and had nothing to offer, but there was a chicken that had been carrying a child for over ten days and was about to be killed.
  • 祖録事不得名早出见担鵞向市中者鵞见録事频顾而鸣 - An unnamed official named Zu saw a goose being carried to the market, and the goose kept looking at him and calling.
  • 祖乃以钱赎之至僧寺令放为长生鵞竟不肯入寺但走逐祖 - Zu bought the goose and took it to a temple to release it as a long-lived goose, but the goose refused to enter the temple and followed him instead.
  • 后经坊歴市稠人广众之处一步不放 - The goose followed him through the streets and markets, never letting go.
  • 左丞张锡亲见说也 - Left Chancellor Zhang Xi personally saw this happen.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 聲稱 - In this context, it means 'calling out' or 'announcing.'

  • 不可盡 - Means 'cannot be exhausted.'
  • 通州界内多獭 - Refers to many otters in Tongzhou.

  • 插雉尾于獭穴前 - Means 'inserting a pheasant's tail in front of the otter's den.'
  • 上岸 - Means 'going ashore.'
  • 奪之 - Means 'taking them.'
  • 放令自吃 - Means 'letting them eat on their own.'
  • 飽即鳴杖以驅之 - Means 'when full, they beat a stick to drive them away.'
  • 有人見竖子在洛水中洗馬 - Describes someone seeing a child washing a horse in the Luo River.
  • 見一物如白練帶 - Refers to seeing something like a white silk ribbon.
  • 極光晶 - Means 'extremely bright and shiny.'
  • 繳 - Means 'to wrap around.'
  • 項三兩匝 - Means 'wrapped around the neck three or four times.'
  • 落水死 - Means 'fell into the water and died.'
  • 人澡浴洗馬死者皆謂鼋所引 - Means 'people who bathe or wash horses and die are said to be led by a turtle.'
  • 非也 - Means 'not so.'
  • 此名白特 - Refers to this being called 'White Te.'
  • 宜慎防之 - Means 'should be cautious.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 漢時 - Refers to the Han Dynasty.
  • 漢末 - Refers to the end of the Han Dynasty.

  • 秦吉了鳥 - An ancient Chinese name for the Asian koel, a type of cuckoo bird known for its ability to mimic human speech.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 咸亨中 - Reign title of Emperor Gaozong of Tang (669–674 CE); indicates the time period when the event occurred.
  • 持鉢乞食 - Literally 'holding a begging bowl to beg for food'; a common phrase for monks or ascetics who beg for alms.
  • 邨人長者 - 'Village elder' or 'respected villager'; a term of respect for an older, respected member of the community.
  • 施以蔬供 - 'Gave him a vegetarian meal'; refers to the act of offering food to a monk or ascetic.
  • 漁人得一魚 - 'Fishermen caught a fish'; the character  is likely a variant or corrupted form of 捕 ('to catch').
  • 長六七尺 - 'Six or seven chi long'; a chi (尺) is an ancient Chinese unit of length, roughly 30–33 cm, so this fish was about 1.8–2.3 meters long.
  • 絲鱗镂甲 - 'Silken scales, engraved armor'; a poetic or metaphorical description of the fish’s appearance.
  • 锦质宝章 - 'Silken quality, precious patterns'; suggests the fish’s body was as fine and beautiful as silk with ornate markings.
  • 特异常鱼 - 'Especially unusual fish'; indicates the fish was not ordinary.
  • 赍赴州饷遗至邨而死 - 'Carried to the state for presentation, but died on the way back to the village'; indicates the fish was taken to the local government as a gift.
  • 剖而分之 - 'Cut it open and divided it'; refers to the villagers cutting up the fish.
  • 得长者所施蔬食俨然并在 - 'They found the vegetables and food the elder had given, still intact'; indicates the food was preserved inside the fish.
  • 设斋超度 - 'Set up a vegetarian meal to perform a Buddhist rite for the deceased'; a religious act to help the soul of the deceased move on.
  • 无水族至今犹然 - 'No water creatures have been seen since, and it is still the case'; indicates the miraculous event led to a lasting change in the lake.
  • 掘井才深五六尺 - 'Dug a well only five or six chi deep'; a very shallow well.
  • 土中得鱼数十头 - 'Found dozens of fish in the soil'; indicates fish were found buried in the earth.
  • 土有防润 - 'The soil had moisture and fertility'; suggests the soil was rich or well-watered.
  • 贞观中 - A reign title of Emperor Taizong of Tang (627–649 CE); indicates the time period of the second story.
  • 卫州板桥店 - 'Board Bridge Inn in Weizhou'; a specific location in the Weizhou area.
  • 三卫 - A military unit or group of three guards; likely refers to soldiers or guards.
  • 五更早发 - 'Departed early in the fifth watch'; refers to a very early departure time in the night.
  • 夜有人取三卫刀杀张迪 - 'At night, someone took the three guards’ knife and killed Zhang Di'; indicates a murder committed using a weapon belonging to the guards.
  • 刀却内鞘中 - 'The knife was returned to its sheath'; suggests the killer returned the knife to its original place.
  • 贞等不知之 - 'Zhen et al. did not know about it'; indicates the guards were unaware of the murder.
  • 至明店人趋贞等拔刀血狼藉 - 'At dawn, the innkeeper rushed to Zhen et al., who drew their swords and blood was everywhere'; indicates the guards were arrested and found with blood on them.
  • 囚禁拷讯 - 'Imprisoned and interrogated'; refers to the legal process of questioning suspects.
  • 苦毒遂自诬 - 'Under torture, they confessed falsely'; indicates the guards were forced to confess to a crime they did not commit.
  • 上疑之 - 'The emperor suspected it'; indicates the emperor doubted the verdict.
  • 差御史蒋恒覆推 - 'Sent the imperial inspector Jiang Heng to re-investigate'; indicates the case was re-examined by a higher authority.
  • 总追店人十五以上集为人不足且散 - 'He ordered the gathering of all store people over fifteen years old, but the number was insufficient and they scattered'; indicates the investigation failed to find enough witnesses.
  • 惟留一老婆年八十已上 - 'Only one elderly woman over eighty years old was left'; indicates the only witness was an old woman.
  • 婆出当有一人与婆语者即记取姓名勿令漏泄 - 'When the old woman came out, if someone spoke with her, record their name and do not let it leak'; indicates the plan to identify the person who spoke with her.
  • 果有一人共语者即记之 - 'Indeed, there was one person who spoke with her, and they recorded their name'; indicates the person was identified.
  • 明日复尔其人又问婆使人作何推勘 - 'The next day, the same thing happened, and the person asked the old woman, 'What is the investigation like?''; indicates the person was trying to learn more about the investigation.
  • 如是者二日并是此人 - 'Like this for two days, and it was always this person'; indicates the person was identified as the culprit.
  • 恒总追集男女三百余人就中唤与老婆语者一人出余并放散 - 'Heng gathered over 300 men and women, and from them, called out the one who spoke with the old woman; the rest were released'; indicates the investigation narrowed down to the one person.
  • 问之具伏云与迪妻奸杀有实 - 'When questioned, he confessed and said, 'I had an affair with Zhang Di’s wife and killed him'; indicates the person admitted to the crime.
  • 奏之敕赐帛二百段除侍御史 - 'He reported it, and the emperor granted 200 bolts of silk and appointed him as a Censor'; indicates the person was rewarded for his role in solving the case.

  • 纳言曰道是夜书表状何故白日里不识宰相 - Means 'the chancellor said, 'if he wrote a memorial at night, why didn't he recognize the chancellor during the day?'
  • 令大惭曰愿纳言莫说向宰相 - Means 'the magistrate was greatly ashamed and said, 'please, chancellor, don't tell the emperor.'
  • 纳言曰师徳已上马与公料理往呼驿长责曰判官与纳言何到不与供给索杖来驿长惶怖拜伏 - Means 'the chancellor said, 'Chancellor Shide has already mounted his horse, let me handle it for you.   • 纳言曰我欲打汝一顿大使打驿将纳碎事徒涴却名声若你州县道你即不存生命且放却驿将跪拜流汗狼狈而走 - Means 'the chancellor said, 'I wanted to beat you, but the大使 (ambassador) would beat the驿将 (postmaster) to death. Idiomatic Expressions

  • 道士史崇懐州河内县缝靴人也 - Formal introduction of a Taoist named Shi Chongxuan, originally from Huai Prefecture's Heine County, who was a cobbler.
  • 后度为道士侨假人也 - Indicates that he later became a Taoist, implying a transformation in his life.
  • 附太平为太清观主 - Suggests that he aligned himself with the 'Great Peace' (太平) and became the head of the Taqing Temple.
  • 金仙玉真出俗立为尊师 - Refers to the deities Gold Immortal and Jade Truth, who are mentioned in the context of him being elevated to the status of a revered teacher.
  • 每入内奏请赏赐甚厚无物不赐 - Describes the generous rewards he received for his services.
  • 授鸿胪卿衣紫罗裙帔握象笏佩鱼符 - Details the official attire and symbols of his high rank.
  • 出入禁闱公私避路 - Indicates the respect and deference shown to him by both officials and commoners.
  • 神武斩之京中士女相贺 - Describes the execution of a person named Shenwu and the public celebration that followed.
  • 岭南风俗家有人病先杀鸡鵞等以祀之 - Describes a local custom in southern China where people sacrifice chickens, geese, and other animals to appease illness.
  • 将为修福若不差即次杀猪狗以祈之 - Continues the description of the sacrificial rituals, moving from chickens and geese to pigs and dogs.
  • 不差即次杀太牢以祷之 - Further escalation of the sacrifices, involving the most prestigious offering, the 'Tai Lao' (a bull, sheep, and pig).
  • 更不差即是命不复更祈死则打鼓鸣钟于堂 - Indicates that if the sacrifices do not work, the person is considered to be beyond help and is subjected to a ritualistic death by drumming and ringing of bells.
  • 比至讫初死且走大呌而哭 - Describes the funeral rites and the initial mourning process.
  • 景云中有长发贺景自称五戒贤者 - Introduces a figure named He Xuanjing, who claims to be a 'Five Precepts Sage' during the Jingyun era.
  • 同为妖者十余人于陆浑山中结草舎幻惑愚人子女 - Describes a group of about ten people, including He Xuanjing, who set up a makeshift shelter in Lu Hun Mountain to deceive simple-minded people and their children.
  • 倾家产事之 - Indicates that people spent all their wealth to follow these deceivers.
  • 绐云至心求者必得成佛 - Describes the deception that those who sincerely seek will achieve Buddhahood.
  • 景为金簿袈裟独坐暗室 - Describes He Xuanjing wearing a golden robe and sitting alone in a dark room.
  • 令愚者窃视云佛放光众皆摄伏 - Describes how he tricked the simple-minded into believing that he was emitting divine light.

  • 行骤脚不散 - Means 'it runs quickly and its hooves do not scatter'; translated as 'it runs quickly and its hooves do not scatter.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 先天年 - The era name 'Tianxian' was used during the Tang Dynasty, specifically during the reign of Emperor Ruizong.
  • 洛下人 - Luoyang was a major city in Chinese history, known for its cultural and political significance.
  • 隋文皇帝 - Emperor Wen of Sui was the founder of the Sui Dynasty, known for his administrative reforms and military conquests.
  • 大宛国 - The Da Yuan (Fergana) kingdom was located in Central Asia and was known for its horses.
  • 千里马 - A thousand-mile horse is a legendary horse known for its speed and endurance.
  • 唐文武圣皇帝 - This refers to Emperor Taizong of Tang, known for his military prowess and governance.
  • 同州刺史宇文士及 - Yuwen Shi was a prominent official during the Tang Dynasty.

  • 挠硙 - This term refers to a type of mill used for grinding grain.
  • 尾焦秃皮内穿穴 - Describes the physical condition of the horse, indicating it was old and worn out.
  • 张纳之 - Zhang Na was a historical figure, possibly a military leader or official.
  • 白马 - A white horse, often symbolizing purity and nobility in Chinese culture.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 忽生一子深目而高鼻 - Literal description of a child with deep-set eyes and a high nose, indicating a different racial or ethnic background.
  • 疑其非嗣将不举 - Phrase expressing doubt about the legitimacy of the child and the intention to abandon him.
  • 赤草马生一白驹 - Metaphorical expression where a red-grass horse gives birth to a white colt, symbolizing an unexpected or miraculous event.
  • 吾曾祖貌胡 - Reference to the speaker's great-grandfather having a 'Hun' appearance, indicating a mixed heritage.
  • 遂养之故曰白马活胡儿 - Phrase explaining that the child was adopted, hence the name 'White Horse Saves the Hun Child.'
  • 蛇丘地险多渐洳 - Description of a dangerous and swampy area where snakes live.
  • 蛇或有人头而蛇身 - Description of a snake with a human head and a snake body, indicating a mythical creature.
  • 岭南有报寃蛇 - Reference to a snake in the southern regions that can cause harm if provoked.
  • 将蜈蚣自防乃免 - Suggestion that carrying a centipede can protect against the snake's attack.
  • 顾渚山頳石洞有緑蛇 - Description of a green snake living in a red stone cave in Gu Zhu Mountain.
  • 好栖树杪 - Phrase indicating that the snake likes to rest at the tips of trees.
  • 视之若鞶带纒于柯叶间 - Metaphorical description of the snake's appearance, likening it to a belt wrapped around branches and leaves.
  • 无螫毒见人则空中飞 - Description of a snake that is not venomous and flies away when it sees people.
  • 山南五溪黔中皆有毒蛇 - Statement about the presence of venomous snakes in the southern regions and the five streams of Qian.
  • 乌而反鼻 - Description of a snake with a black color and a reversed snout.
  • 蟠于草中 - Phrase indicating that the snake coils in the grass.
  • 其牙倒勾 - Description of the snake's teeth, which are curved backward.
  • 去人数步直来疾如缴箭 - Phrase describing the snake's sudden and rapid movement towards people.
  • 螫人立死 - Statement about the snake's deadly bite.
  • 中手即断手中足即断足 - Description of the snake's venom causing immediate amputation of limbs.
  • 不然则全身肿烂 - Phrase indicating that if the snake's bite does not cause immediate death, it leads to severe swelling and decay.
  • 百无一活 - Statement about the high mortality rate of those bitten by the snake.
  • 谓蝮蛇也 - Phrase identifying the snake as a venomous one.
  • 黄喉蛇好在舎上 - Description of a yellow-throated snake that likes to live on rooftops.
  • 无毒不害人 - Statement about the snake being non-venomous and harmless.
  • 唯善食毒蛇 - Phrase indicating that the snake primarily feeds on other venomous snakes.
  • 食饱垂头直下滴沬地喷起变为沙虱 - Description of the snake's behavior after eating, where it drools and transforms into sand fleas.
  • 中人为疾额上有大王字 - Description of the symptoms of being bitten by the snake, including a large 'king' character on the forehead.
  • 众蛇之长常食蝮蛇 - Statement about the snake being the leader among snakes and often preying on venomous ones.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 常有苍鹘集重阁 - Literal meaning: 'often there are gray hawks gathering on the high pavilion.' Describes a recurring natural phenomenon.
  • 每有鸽数千 - Literal meaning: 'each time there are thousands of birds.' Indicates a large number of birds appearing regularly.
  • 冬中每夕取一鸽以暖足 - Literal meaning: 'in winter, each night they take one dove to warm their feet.' Suggests a specific behavior of the hawks during winter.
  • 至晓放之而不杀 - Literal meaning: 'until dawn, they release it without killing it.' Implies a non-lethal interaction between the hawks and the doves.
  • 自余鹰鹘不敢侮之 - Literal meaning: 'other eagles and hawks dare not bully them.' Indicates a hierarchical relationship among the birds.
  • 取鸟常驱至于殿前然后击杀 - Literal meaning: 'when catching birds, they often drive them to the front of the hall and then kill them.' Describes a method of hunting birds.
  • 名落鴈殿 - Literal meaning: 'named the Goose-Hunting Hall.' A specific name for a hall used for hunting.
  • 象入庄家中庭卧其足下有槎人为出之 - Literal meaning: 'an elephant entered the village and lay in the courtyard, with its feet under the tree; the people drove it out.' Describes an unusual event involving an elephant.
  • 以鼻豗土得象牙数十以报之 - Literal meaning: 'using its trunk to dig up soil, it found dozens of ivory tusks and returned them.' Indicates the elephant's actions and the reward it received.
  • 吏部侍郎郑愔初托附来俊臣俊臣诛即托张易之易之被戮托韦庶人后附谯王竟被斩 - Literal meaning: 'Counselor of the Ministry of Personnel Zheng Yin initially relied on Lai Junchen; when Lai was executed, he relied on Zhang Yizhi; when Zhang was killed, he relied on Wei Shuren; later he attached himself to the Prince of Zhao, and was eventually executed.' Describes Zheng Yin's changing allegiances and ultimate fate.
  • 太子少保薛稷雍州长史李晋中书令崔湜萧志忠岑羲等并外饰忠鯾内藏谄媚翕肩屛气防痔折肢附太平公主并腾迁云路咸自以为得志保泰山之安七月三日破家身斩何异鹓鸯栖于苇苕大风忽起巢折卵破后之君子可不鍳哉 - Literal meaning: 'Grand Tutor Xue Ji, Prefect of Yongzhou Li Jin, Chancellor Cui Shi, Xiao Zhizhong, and Cen Xi, among others, all outwardly displayed loyalty but inwardly harbored flattery, bowing their shoulders and holding their breath to avoid trouble. They supported Princess Taiping and rose to high positions, believing they were secure. On July 3rd, they were destroyed, their families broken, and they were executed. How different is this from a phoenix perched on reeds, where a sudden strong wind causes the nest to fall and the eggs to break. Future scholars can reflect on this, can they not?' Describes the corrupt behavior of several high-ranking officials and their eventual downfall.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 谄事 - To flatter and serve, often implying servility or sycophancy.

  • 折支势族 - To undermine or weaken influential families or clans.
  • 防痔权门 - To avoid or prevent offenses against powerful families, metaphorically referring to avoiding trouble with those in power.
  • 猛若饥虎 - Describes someone as fierce as a hungry tiger.

  • 性爱食人 - Literally means 'loves to eat people,' but is likely a metaphor for being extremely greedy or voracious.
  • 终为人所食 - Literally means 'eventually eaten by others,' but is likely a metaphor for being destroyed or punished by others.
  • 定昆池 - Refers to a man-made lake, symbolizing grandiose projects or ambitions.

  • 斜褰紫衫为公主背挽金犊车 - Describes a scene where Sīnóng Sī lifts his purple robe and walks behind Princess Anle while pulling a golden ox-drawn carriage, symbolizing his servility and the princess's dominance.
  • 诛逆韦之际 - Refers to the time when the rebels were being punished.
  • 上御承天门 - Means 'the emperor stood at the Chengtian Gate.'
  • 诈喜舞蹈称万岁 - Describes the act of pretending to be happy, dancing, and shouting 'Long live the emperor!'
  • 刀劎乱下 - Describes a chaotic scene of swords falling.
  • 与男同戮 - Means 'was executed along with his son.'
  • 割一脔肉骨俱尽 - Describes the gruesome act of cutting off a piece of flesh until only bones remained.
  • 天后时 - Refers to the time of Empress Wu Zetian.
  • 掌擎黄幞 - Means 'holding a yellow silk canopy.'
  • 随薛师后 - Means 'following after Master Xue.'
  • 伏地承薛师马镫 - Describes the act of lying on the ground to hold Master Xue's horse stirrups.
  • 偷媚取容 - Means 'to flatter and seek favor.'
  • 实名教之大也 - Means 'this is indeed a great evil in the teachings of morality.'
  • 犊车载入 - Describes the act of being taken into the carriage.
  • 三日不语 - Means 'three days without speaking.'
  • 儿父犯国法忧之无复聊赖 - Means 'my father committed a crime against the state, and I am worried and have no more hope.'
  • 既幸免其父极刑 - Means 'since he was fortunate to escape his father's extreme punishment.'
  • 遂进顼笼马监 - Means 'then promoted Xu to the position of horsekeeper.'
  • 俄迁中丞吏部侍郎 - Means 'soon promoted Xu to the position of Censor-in-Chief and Deputy Minister of Personnel.'
  • 不以才升 - Means 'not promoted based on talent.'
  • 二妹请求承嗣故也 - Means 'because the two sisters requested Chengsi.'

  • 遂为作传二卷论薛师之圣从天而降 - Suggests that Zong Chuke wrote a two-volume biography praising Xue Shi, claiming he was a divine being sent from heaven.

  • 期年之间位至内史 - Indicates that within a year, Xue Shi rose to the position of Naishi (an important official).
  • 天武梁王武三思为张易之作传云是王子晋后身 - States that Wu Sansi, a prince, wrote a biography of Zhang Yizhi, claiming he was the reincarnation of Prince Ziqing (a historical figure).
  • 于缑氏山立庙词人才子佞者为诗以咏之 - Describes how poets and flatterers wrote poems praising Zhang Yizhi, and a temple was built for him on Mount Gou.

  • 崔湜谄事张易之与韦庶人及韦氏诛附太平有冯子都董偃之宠 - Describes Cui Shi, who flattered Zhang Yizhi and later allied with Empress Wei and her family, gaining favor similar to that of Feng Zidu and Dong Yan.
  • 妻美与二女并进储闱为中书侍郎平事 - Indicates that Cui Shi's wife and two daughters entered the imperial harem, and he became a high-ranking official.
  • 后毛仲巡边防说于天雄军大设酒酣恩敕忽降授兵部尚书同中书门下三品 - Indicates that after Wang Maozhong went on a border inspection, Zhang Yue was promoted to a high position at a banquet.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • This long sentence describes the brother's physical abilities and a specific action. It can be broken down into: 'The brother of Chai Shao had extraordinary physical abilities, agility, and speed. He leaped up and stood straight as if flying, covering more than ten steps before stopping.'
  • This sentence can be broken down into: 'The emperor ordered to fetch the saddle and stirrups of Grand Duke Changsun Wuji and also ordered to notify Changsun Wuji to prepare for defense.'
  • This sentence describes a supernatural event: 'At night, an object like a bird flew into the house, cutting off enemies and leaving.'
  • This sentence describes a similar supernatural event: 'The emperor ordered to fetch the gold-inlaid pillow case of Princess Danyang. The object flew into the room and touched Princess Danyang's face, then lifted his head and replaced it with another pillow before leaving.'
  • This long sentence describes a series of supernatural feats: 'By dawn, it was noticed that he had been wearing Gimo boots and walking on the brick wall, reaching the parapet without any support. He stepped on the column of a Buddhist hall and reached the roof, crossing over a hundred-foot tower with no obstacles.'
  • This sentence can be broken down into: 'The emperor found this amazing and said, 'This person cannot be stationed in the capital; he should be sent out as an official.' People at the time called him the Wall Dragon.'
  • This sentence describes a theft: 'The emperor once gave Changsun Wuji a seven-jewel belt worth a thousand gold pieces. At that time, a major thief named Duan Shizi descended from the roof through a beam hole, revealing his sword and saying, 'If you move, you will die.' He took the belt and jumped out through the beam hole, supported by his sword.'

[131]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 魏征为仆射有二典事之长参时 - Hui Zheng, as the Grand Secretary, had two officials who were his attendants during his time.
  • 官职禄料由天者葢不虚也 - The saying that one's official position and salary are determined by heaven is indeed true.
  • 娄师徳为杨州江都尉冯元常亦为尉共见张冏藏 - Lou Shide served as the magistrate of Yangzhou's Jiangdu County, while Feng Yuanchang also served as a magistrate and they both met Zhang Junzang.
  • 冯位不如娄冯唯取钱多即官益进 - Feng's rank was lower than Lou's, but he took more bribes and thus advanced more quickly.
  • 娄若取一钱官即落后 - If Lou took even one coin, his position would be lower.
  • 王显与文武皇帝有严子陵之旧 - Wang Xian had a close friendship with Emperor Wenwu, similar to the relationship between Yan Ziling and Emperor Guangwu.
  • 王显抵老不作蠒 - Wang Xian, as he grew old, did not become a silkworm.
  • 朝贵而夕死足矣 - It is enough to be honored in the morning and die in the evening.
  • 取紫袍金带赐之 - He was given a purple robe and a golden belt.
  • 其夜卒 - He died that night.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 魏征 - A famous advisor and historian during the Tang Dynasty.
  • 仆射 - A high-ranking official in the Tang Dynasty, often translated as Grand Secretary.
  • 典事之长参 - Attendants or subordinates of the Grand Secretary.
  • 张冏藏 - A person who met both Lou Shide and Feng Yuanchang.
  • 娄师徳 - A Tang Dynasty official known for his integrity.
  • 冯元常 - Another Tang Dynasty official, less virtuous than Lou Shide.
  • 杨州江都尉 - A position in the Tang Dynasty, referring to the magistrate of Yangzhou's Jiangdu County.
  • 文武皇帝 - Refers to Emperor Wenwu of Tang.
  • 严子陵 - A famous recluse and friend of Emperor Guangwu of Han.
  • 紫袍金带 - Purple robes and golden belts were symbols of high rank and honor in the Tang Dynasty.
  • 蠒 - A silkworm, often used metaphorically to describe someone who becomes prosperous.

Proper Nouns

  • 仆射 - Grand Secretary
  • 典事之长参 - Attendants

  • 娄师徳 - Lou Shide

  • 文武皇帝 - Emperor Wenwu of Tang
  • 严子陵 - Yan Ziling
  • 紫袍金带 - Purple robe and golden belt
  • 蠒 - Silkworm

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 太宗極康豫 - Describes Emperor Taizong's state of health, implying he was in good health.
  • 太史令淳风见上流泪无言 - A classical expression where the historian sighs without speaking, indicating deep emotion.
  • 陛下夕当晏驾 - A euphemistic way of saying 'the emperor will die tonight.'
  • 人生有命亦何忧也 - A philosophical statement suggesting that life is predetermined and thus there is no need to worry.
  • 留淳风宿 - Indicates that Emperor Taizong kept the historian overnight.
  • 至夜半奄然入定 - Describes the emperor's sudden death during the night.
  • 陛下蹔合来还即去也 - A ghostly message suggesting that the emperor will return briefly but then leave.
  • 生人判防事 - Refers to a ghostly official in charge of judgment.
  • 六月四日事 - Likely refers to a specific historical event, possibly related to Emperor Taizong's reign.
  • 文武圣皇帝微时与无防蒱戏争彩 - Describes a pastime involving gambling, with '微时' indicating a time when Emperor Taizong was not yet an emperor.
  • 李阳之宿憾焉 - Refers to a long-standing grudge or resentment.
  • 登极防藏匿不出 - Describes someone hiding after Emperor Taizong ascended the throne.
  • 索钱二十千 - Indicates a request for a large sum of money.
  • 酧钱十八贯 - Describes the amount of money given in return.
  • 春明门待诸州麻车三日 - Describes a period of waiting at the Chuning Gate for the arrival of state vehicles.
  • 属灞桥破唯得麻三车更无所有 - Indicates that only three carts of hemp were obtained due to the destruction of the Ba Bridge.
  • 官皆由天也 - Suggests that one's fate is determined by heaven.
  • 朝野佥载卷六 - The title of the text, indicating it is part of a collection of anecdotes and stories.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 太宗 - Emperor Taizong of Tang, one of the most famous emperors of the Tang Dynasty.
  • 太史令淳风 - Li Chunfeng, a renowned astronomer and historian of the Tang Dynasty.
  • 晏驾 - A euphemism for the death of an emperor.
  • 生人判防事 - A ghostly official in charge of judgment, reflecting traditional beliefs in the afterlife.
  • 六月四日事 - Could refer to a significant event, such as a military campaign or a political crisis.
  • 文武圣皇帝 - A title given to Emperor Taizong, emphasizing his wisdom and military prowess.
  • 无防 - A person mentioned in the text, possibly a friend or associate of Emperor Taizong.
  • 李阳 - A person with a long-standing grudge against Emperor Taizong.
  • 登极 - The act of ascending the throne.
  • 春明门 - A gate in the capital city, often used as a reference point in historical texts.

  • 朝野佥载 - A collection of historical anecdotes and stories from the Tang Dynasty.
